description: In December 2009, professional golfer Tiger Woods faced massive public scrutiny following revelations of his extramarital affairs. Global consulting firm Accenture, which featured Woods in a prominent 'High Performance' advertising campaign, became a focal point of the crisis, leading to questions about whether brands should drop celebrity partners during personal scandals.

key_events.description: National Enquirer breaks the story of the affairs.
Tiger Woods announces an indefinite break from golf.
Accenture officially drops Tiger Woods from their marketing campaign.
Public sentiment solidifies that corporate association with infidelity is a liability.

calibration_notes: This scenario serves as a foundational case for measuring brand risk in the pre-social media era, illustrating how quickly public moral judgment forces corporate decoupling from high-value endorsements.
